question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: - The HR org chart update most likely changed identity/group mappings which cascaded into three separate failure modes: (A) a cloud vs on prem group / Azure AD Connect collision that removed guest/freelancer membership from the SharePoint ACLs and caused the “locked out” errors; (B) automated provisioning mapped Sales users into the tenant “Global” voice routing policy (which has no PSTN Usage) so outbound PSTN calls failed (beep → drop); and (C) the NEW_FINANCE group became nested under the ZURICH location AD group so Zurich location GPOs (default printer MFP ZUERICH 01) flowed to Basel Finance users and confidential invoices printed in the wrong office. Each of those mechanisms is described and reproduced in your incident records

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 64: # Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint) This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)] REPORT 48: # Teams Direct Routing — Sales Users, Global vs DR-Standard Voice Routing A subset of roughly six Sales users who were configured for Direct Routing experienced loss of outbound PSTN calling because a provisioning script assigned them the "Global" voice routing policy which did not reference any PSTN Usage (External-Route). DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route" and assigning DR-Standard restored external calling in tests. The internal blueprint specifies Direct Routing users must receive a dedicated routing policy (e.g., DR-Standard/DR-Sales), indicating the provisioning behavior deviated from the documented standard. Additionally, the Sales team experienced a separate mail flow issue for older Outlook clients that was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users, showing multiple recent configuration exceptions for the same user subset. [Data: Entities (203, 193, 194, 201, 197), Relationships (301, 303, 304, 305, 300)] ## Root cause — provisioning script assigned the Global policy lacking PSTN Usage Investigation shows the provisioning script was assigning the "Global" voice routing policy by default to users who became members of the Enterprise-Sales group; that default assignment is the misconfiguration that prevented external routing for Direct Routing-enabled users. The scripting behavior is explicitly documented in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to the PROVISIONING SCRIPT [Data: Entities (193), Relationships (301)]. The Global policy observed did not reference any PSTN Usage (including the required External-Route), so users assigned to it had no external PSTN path and outbound calls failed. This absence of PSTN Usage in the Global policy is recorded in the relationship that ties VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (193, 196), Relationships (303)]. ## Scope and impact — ~6 Sales users with Direct Routing lost outbound calling Approximately six users in the Sales team who were configured for Direct Routing could not place external PSTN calls because they were assigned the Global policy without PSTN Usage. The affected user group is explicitly identified as "AFFECTED USERS (ABOUT 6 USERS)" and is linked to Direct Routing configuration in the dataset [Data: Entities (203, 197), Relationships (300, 307)]. While the immediate count is small (~6 users), the issue affected production voice service for those users and required targeted remediation (policy reassignment/testing). The records document that these users were Direct Routing-enabled and therefore required a routing policy that provided an external PSTN path [Data: Entities (203), Relationships (300)]. ## DR-Standard provides the required PSTN route and temporarily restored calling DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route", which provides the external PSTN path necessary for outbound dialing by Direct Routing users. This is captured in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: DR-STANDARD to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]. Operational testing showed temporarily assigning DR-Standard to an affected user restored external calling, indicating the absence of External-Route in the Global policy was the proximate technical blocker to outbound calling for the impacted users [Data: Entities (194), Relationships (304)]. ## Design/standards mismatch — provisioning behavior deviated from the blueprint The internal blueprint (BLUEPRINT – TEAMS VOICE ROUTING & POLICY ASSIGNMENT – VERSION 1.3) documents that Direct Routing users must be assigned a dedicated routing policy (for example DR-Standard or DR-Sales) rather than a global/default policy. This expected assignment is explicitly referenced in the relationship between DR-Standard and the blueprint [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305)]. Because the provisioning script defaulted to the Global policy, there is a clear divergence between current automated provisioning and the documented standard — a governance and process gap that allowed the misassignment to occur [Data: Entities (193, 201), Relationships (301, 305)]. ## Recurrence risk — automation propagates the misconfiguration The provisioning script's default assignment behavior represents a systematic failure mode: any user added by that automation to the Enterprise-Sales group may receive the Global policy and therefore lack PSTN routing. The script-to-policy relationship explicitly documents this automated assignment path and is the vector for recurrence unless changed [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (193)]. Because the provisioning script is an automated control point, the misconfiguration can scale beyond the initial ~6 users if left unaddressed, making remediation of the script a higher-priority fix than ad-hoc manual reassignment for individual users [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (203)]. ## Co-occurrence of different operational issues for the same user subset The Sales team (roughly 12 employees) had a subset of about six users who experienced both the Direct Routing outbound call failure and an unrelated mail issue where outgoing mail stuck in older Outlook clients' Outbox; the mail issue was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for those affected users [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. This co-occurrence indicates the same user subset has been subject to multiple configuration exceptions or special-case changes, which suggests either rapid organizational change (reassignment to "Enterprise Sales") or gaps in templated provisioning and policy inheritance that should be reviewed to avoid repeated one-off fixes [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. ## Recommended priority actions — policy reassignment, provisioning update, and audit Immediate actions supported by the evidence: (1) Reassign affected Direct Routing users from the Global policy to a routing policy that references PSTN Usage: External-Route (e.g., DR-Standard) — this action has been shown to restore calling in tests [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]; (2) Update the provisioning script so it assigns the documented dedicated Direct Routing policy rather than Global to Enterprise-Sales members [Data: Entities (201, 193), Relationships (301, 305)]; and (3) Conduct an audit of current voice routing policy assignments for all Direct Routing-enabled users to find additional misassignments [Data: Entities (203, 201), Relationships (300, 305)]. These steps directly address the root cause (script assigning Global) and align operational state with the blueprint (dedicated DR policy), reducing recurrence risk and restoring consistent governance over telephony routing [Data: Relationships (301, 305), Entities (194, 201)]. ## Governance and runbook recommendations — enforce blueprint and add verification The blueprint requires a dedicated routing policy for Direct Routing users; to prevent recurrence, codify that requirement in provisioning templates and add a verification step that checks assigned voice routing policies for presence of a PSTN Usage reference before enabling Direct Routing for a user [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305, 304)]. Operational runbook updates should include: a) a checklist for onboarding Direct Routing users that verifies policy assignment and PSTN Usage presence, b) an automated policy-assignment unit test in the provisioning pipeline, and c) an alert/monitor that flags Direct Routing-enabled accounts assigned to policies with no PSTN Usage. Tying these controls back to the documented blueprint will close the governance loop exposed by the incident [Data: Entities (201, 193, 194), Relationships (301, 305, 304)]. REPORT 136: # Finance AD Group Reorg — Zurich Location GPO / Printer Misassignment A reorganization moved Finance users into a newly created NEW FINANCE AD GROUP while deactivating the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP. The NEW FINANCE AD GROUP had been (inadvertently) a member of a higher-level ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP, which is scoped to apply Zurich-targeted GPOs (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01). Because of this nested membership, multiple Finance users (Basel-based) received the Zurich default printer instead of their intended device; those affected users were tested after remediation and validated as resolved. The event is rooted in AD group membership changes during a reorg and demonstrates a gap in membership validation and GPO-scoping controls. [Data: Relationships (87,89,85,91); Entities (55,56,59,54,62)] ## Root cause: nested AD group membership caused Zurich GPOs (including default printer) to apply to Finance users The NEW FINANCE AD GROUP was a member of the higher-level ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP, which resulted in Zurich-scoped Group Policy Objects (GPOs) — including the default printer mapping to MFP-ZUERICH-01 — being applied to users who were intended to receive Finance-targeted policies. The explicit link between the new Finance group and the Zurich location group is documented as the misconfiguration that allowed location GPOs to flow into Finance users' sessions. [Data: Relationships (87,91); Entities (55,56)] This is a classic nested-group GPO-scoping issue: location-scope group membership was inherited by an organizational/service-focused group, producing side effects (printer mapping) outside the intended boundary. ## Change event sequence: reorganization and group lifecycle change introduced the misconfiguration The Finance department was migrated into a newly created NEW FINANCE AD GROUP during a reorganization, and the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP was deactivated as part of that change. That migration and deactivation sequence is the proximal change event that resulted in unexpected nested membership in the Zurich location group. The timeline indicates the reorg activity as the starting point for the misapplied policies. [Data: Relationships (85,89); Entities (54,59,55)] Because the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP was removed and users were moved into the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP, any unintended parent-group memberships of the new group immediately affected the entire set of migrated users. ## Impact scope: multiple Finance (Basel) users experienced incorrect default printer assignment A set of affected Finance-department employees at the Basel location experienced their default printer automatically switching to the Zurich device. The dataset explicitly identifies these affected users and ties them to the Finance department membership that determines policy application. This shows the incident affected multiple end users within an organizational unit rather than an isolated single-user event. [Data: Entities (62); Relationships (86)] The misapplied printer mapping (Zurich device) is explicitly associated with the Zurich location GPOs, confirming the functional impact on print services for those users. [Data: Relationships (91)] ## GPO scoping is location-based and includes the MFP-ZUERICH-01 default printer assignment The ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP represents the Zurich site/scope in Active Directory and is associated with Zurich-targeted GPOs and the Zurich printer assignment (MFP-ZUERICH-01). This confirms that location AD groups are being used to scope device and printer mappings, so membership in a location group directly controls which printers users receive. [Data: Relationships (91); Entities (56)] Because those GPOs carry device mapping settings, any non-location group that becomes a member of the location group will inherit those device-targeted settings, which is what occurred here. ## Remediation validated: affected users tested and remediation resolved the issue After corrective action, the affected users were tested and validated as resolved according to the dataset. The explicit testing and validation step demonstrates that the corrective change(s) were effective for the identified set of users. [Data: Entities (62)] This confirms that the misconfiguration was functionally reversible and that remediation successfully restored expected printer mappings for the tested users. ## Evidence of governance/process gap around AD group membership and change validation The incident sequence—creation of a NEW FINANCE AD GROUP, deactivation of the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP, and the NEW group’s inadvertent membership in the ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP—indicates there was no effective pre-change validation or post-change membership audit to catch inappropriate nested membership before it impacted users. The dataset documents both the reorg move and the incorrect membership as the causal chain. [Data: Relationships (85,89,87); Entities (55,59)] While the records do not name responsible teams, the technical artifacts show a control gap: AD-group lifecycle changes that can affect location-scoped GPOs require explicit checks (membership review, test cohorts) that were not evidently performed in this case. ## Recurring risk: nested groups and implicit inheritance make similar incidents likely without controls Because the Zurich location group applies critical configuration (GPOs and default printers) and the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP was able to gain membership in that location group, the environment is exposed to future misapplied policies if group nesting is not managed. The specific relationships in the data show how a single nested membership can cause broad policy application to an entire department. [Data: Relationships (87,91)] Left unaddressed, this pattern is a recurring failure mode: organizational-group changes (reorgs, group consolidations) frequently create unexpected inheritance of location- or role-scoped policies. ## Priority recommendations: immediate actions and medium-term governance changes to prevent recurrence Immediate (urgent) actions to perform based on the incident facts: - Audit and correct membership: review NEW FINANCE AD GROUP membership and remove unintended parent/location memberships (verify Zurich membership was removed) to stop further misapplication. The incident specifically identifies the NEW group’s membership in the Zurich location group as the cause. [Data: Relationships (87)] - Verify impacted endpoints/users: confirm the remediation validated for AFFECTED USERS and run a wider scope check across Finance to ensure no remaining Basel users are receiving Zurich printer mappings. The dataset documents tested/validated affected users. [Data: Entities (62); Relationships (86)] Medium-term governance and technical controls to reduce recurrence: - Implement group-membership pre-change validation and post-change audit workflows for AD group lifecycle operations (especially for groups that can be nested into location-scoped groups). - Introduce an explicit policy for separating location-scoped groups from organizational/role groups (and prevent cross-membership) or use security filtering/WMI/GP loopback patterns as appropriate to reduce reliance on group nesting for scoping (the data shows location group membership directly controls printer mappings). [Data: Relationships (91)] - Add a change-window and pilot testing requirement for AD group restructures impacting large user sets, plus a communications plan to notify affected departments (for example, Finance) before and after changes. The reorg that created the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP is the documented change event that triggered the problem. [Data: Relationships (85,89); Entities (54)] These recommendations directly address the causal links recorded in the dataset and are prioritized to stop immediate harm, validate remediation, and close the governance gap that allowed the misconfiguration.

Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)]

The immediate technical issues included misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) and delayed device state synchronization for hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices, which caused Conditional Access (Require compliant device for Finance Resources) to evaluate as not satisfied and block access for some users. Employee 21 authored consolidated ADFS configuration documentation, triggered manual device synchronization during remediation, and adjusted a Conditional Access rule (excluding a SharePoint project folder) to restore access; the same engineer also conducted WAF analysis that identified TLS inspection causing 502 errors and helped apply configuration adjustments that restored external access. The primary entities and relationships are: Employee 21 as the operational owner and author of the ADFS documentation, the ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION itself, the Finance Conditional Access policy, Hybrid Azure AD joined devices and the DEVICE SYNCHRONIZATION process, DEVICE COMPLIANCE STATUS results in Entra ID, and PRIVATE DEVICES which produced additional variability in access outcomes. [Data: Entities (232, 244, 680, 683, 684, 688, 685), Relationships (364, 1189, 1199, 1201, 1206, 1208, 365)] ## Single operational lead (Employee 21) conducted analysis, remediation, and documentation. Employee 21 served as the assigned ticket owner and lead analyst across multiple related incidents, performed remote troubleshooting, coordinated remediation with the Network Team, adjusted Conditional Access, triggered device synchronization, and authored the ADFS configuration notes and root‑cause analysis that identified misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) [Data: Entities (232), Relationships (1189, 365, 364)]. Having a single visible owner accelerated the investigation and produced formal documentation (ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION) and tactical remediations; this centralization is beneficial for rapid response but creates a single person dependency for institutional knowledge unless the documentation and handoff processes are formalized. The existence of authored documentation suggests an opportunity to convert incident knowledge into maintained runbooks and to ensure alternate owners are trained and assigned. ## Root cause for token/MFA failures: misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters. The documented root cause for the JWT/MFA login failures was ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) being misconfigured; Employee 21 authored notes describing the corrected settings and consolidated those into a formal ADFS configuration document intended to align production to TEST‑environment values [Data: Entities (232, 244), Relationships (364)]. This indicates the authentication failure was caused by identity provider configuration drift rather than client or network connectivity alone. The formalization into ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION provides a basis for preventing recurrence if it is enforced via configuration management, automated checks, or periodic reviews against the documented expected values. ## Conditional Access policy interaction with device compliance blocked access to finance resources. The tenant Conditional Access policy titled 'Require compliant device for Finance Resources' evaluated device compliance as a condition; where device compliance status was 'unknown' or stale the policy evaluated as 'not satisfied', preventing access until compliance updated. The policy was adjusted (a SharePoint project folder excluded) as part of remediation to restore access for impacted users [Data: Entities (680, 232), Relationships (1201, 1199)]. This demonstrates a multi‑layered failure mode: identity provider configuration problems coincident with device compliance evaluation delays led to user impact for business‑critical (Finance) resources. The temporary exclusion of a resource from the CA policy is an appropriate tactical mitigation but should be followed by root cause fixes (device sync reliability and ADFS configuration) and a formal risk assessment to determine whether broader policy tuning or exceptions are warranted. ## Hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices experienced delayed/faulty synchronization, causing 'unknown' compliance and access failures. Hybrid Azure AD joined devices experienced faulty or delayed synchronization to Entra ID (6–8 hour lag), leaving device compliance status reported as 'unknown' and delaying Conditional Access evaluation and access restoration for some users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683, 684, 688), Relationships (1206, 1189)]. Delayed device synchronization is a systemic reliability issue for hybrid device management that can repeatedly cause Conditional Access denials for otherwise compliant endpoints. Manual syncs are effective as an incident workaround but indicate the need for monitoring, alerting, and automation around device sync health, plus investigation into root causes (connectivity, AD‑connector health, sync throttling, or service limits) and capacity for remediation. ## Private/personal devices contributed to variability in access outcomes due to non‑managed compliance states. Some users accessed resources from private devices which are more likely to report non‑compliant or 'unknown' device status in Entra ID; this was observed in the incident and contributed to Conditional Access evaluation failures [Data: Entities (685, 688), Relationships (1208)]. Private devices introduce expected variability when CA policies require device compliance. The organization should treat private devices as higher‑risk by design (e.g., restrict finance resource access, require stronger controls, or use just‑in‑time access workflows) and ensure user education and policy clarity to reduce helpdesk load from predictable denial scenarios. ## WAF/TLS inspection caused 502 errors for external access; configuration adjustments restored service. During later work Employee 21 performed WAF analysis and identified TLS inspection as the cause of 502 errors when enabled; they compared the WAF configuration to the internal blueprint and helped apply configuration adjustments that restored external access [Data: Entities (232)]. This is an example of infrastructure security tooling (TLS inspection) interacting poorly with application traffic, producing availability issues. The finding underscores the need to validate WAF/inspection rules against blueprints and application behavior, include regression tests for TLS inspection changes, and maintain a rollback plan for inspection rules that disrupt production traffic. ## Documentation exists but should be operationalized into governance, monitoring, and runbooks. A consolidated ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION was recorded to capture the corrected ADFS client registration and investigation details; Employee 21 confirmed the configuration and notes have been documented to avoid future problems [Data: Entities (244), Relationships (364)]. Documentation is a necessary first step; to reduce operational risk this content should be converted into enforceable controls: baseline configuration templates, configuration drift detection (CI/CD or configuration management), automated compliance checks, runbooks for manual and automated remediation (including device sync triggers), and clearly mapped escalation/owner lists so incidents do not rely on a single individual's presence.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 48: # Teams Direct Routing — Sales Users, Global vs DR-Standard Voice Routing A subset of roughly six Sales users who were configured for Direct Routing experienced loss of outbound PSTN calling because a provisioning script assigned them the "Global" voice routing policy which did not reference any PSTN Usage (External-Route). DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route" and assigning DR-Standard restored external calling in tests. The internal blueprint specifies Direct Routing users must receive a dedicated routing policy (e.g., DR-Standard/DR-Sales), indicating the provisioning behavior deviated from the documented standard. Additionally, the Sales team experienced a separate mail flow issue for older Outlook clients that was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users, showing multiple recent configuration exceptions for the same user subset. [Data: Entities (203, 193, 194, 201, 197), Relationships (301, 303, 304, 305, 300)] ## Root cause — provisioning script assigned the Global policy lacking PSTN Usage Investigation shows the provisioning script was assigning the "Global" voice routing policy by default to users who became members of the Enterprise-Sales group; that default assignment is the misconfiguration that prevented external routing for Direct Routing-enabled users. The scripting behavior is explicitly documented in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to the PROVISIONING SCRIPT [Data: Entities (193), Relationships (301)]. The Global policy observed did not reference any PSTN Usage (including the required External-Route), so users assigned to it had no external PSTN path and outbound calls failed. This absence of PSTN Usage in the Global policy is recorded in the relationship that ties VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (193, 196), Relationships (303)]. ## Scope and impact — ~6 Sales users with Direct Routing lost outbound calling Approximately six users in the Sales team who were configured for Direct Routing could not place external PSTN calls because they were assigned the Global policy without PSTN Usage. The affected user group is explicitly identified as "AFFECTED USERS (ABOUT 6 USERS)" and is linked to Direct Routing configuration in the dataset [Data: Entities (203, 197), Relationships (300, 307)]. While the immediate count is small (~6 users), the issue affected production voice service for those users and required targeted remediation (policy reassignment/testing). The records document that these users were Direct Routing-enabled and therefore required a routing policy that provided an external PSTN path [Data: Entities (203), Relationships (300)]. ## DR-Standard provides the required PSTN route and temporarily restored calling DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route", which provides the external PSTN path necessary for outbound dialing by Direct Routing users. This is captured in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: DR-STANDARD to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]. Operational testing showed temporarily assigning DR-Standard to an affected user restored external calling, indicating the absence of External-Route in the Global policy was the proximate technical blocker to outbound calling for the impacted users [Data: Entities (194), Relationships (304)]. ## Design/standards mismatch — provisioning behavior deviated from the blueprint The internal blueprint (BLUEPRINT – TEAMS VOICE ROUTING & POLICY ASSIGNMENT – VERSION 1.3) documents that Direct Routing users must be assigned a dedicated routing policy (for example DR-Standard or DR-Sales) rather than a global/default policy. This expected assignment is explicitly referenced in the relationship between DR-Standard and the blueprint [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305)]. Because the provisioning script defaulted to the Global policy, there is a clear divergence between current automated provisioning and the documented standard — a governance and process gap that allowed the misassignment to occur [Data: Entities (193, 201), Relationships (301, 305)]. ## Recurrence risk — automation propagates the misconfiguration The provisioning script's default assignment behavior represents a systematic failure mode: any user added by that automation to the Enterprise-Sales group may receive the Global policy and therefore lack PSTN routing. The script-to-policy relationship explicitly documents this automated assignment path and is the vector for recurrence unless changed [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (193)]. Because the provisioning script is an automated control point, the misconfiguration can scale beyond the initial ~6 users if left unaddressed, making remediation of the script a higher-priority fix than ad-hoc manual reassignment for individual users [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (203)]. ## Co-occurrence of different operational issues for the same user subset The Sales team (roughly 12 employees) had a subset of about six users who experienced both the Direct Routing outbound call failure and an unrelated mail issue where outgoing mail stuck in older Outlook clients' Outbox; the mail issue was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for those affected users [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. This co-occurrence indicates the same user subset has been subject to multiple configuration exceptions or special-case changes, which suggests either rapid organizational change (reassignment to "Enterprise Sales") or gaps in templated provisioning and policy inheritance that should be reviewed to avoid repeated one-off fixes [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. ## Recommended priority actions — policy reassignment, provisioning update, and audit Immediate actions supported by the evidence: (1) Reassign affected Direct Routing users from the Global policy to a routing policy that references PSTN Usage: External-Route (e.g., DR-Standard) — this action has been shown to restore calling in tests [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]; (2) Update the provisioning script so it assigns the documented dedicated Direct Routing policy rather than Global to Enterprise-Sales members [Data: Entities (201, 193), Relationships (301, 305)]; and (3) Conduct an audit of current voice routing policy assignments for all Direct Routing-enabled users to find additional misassignments [Data: Entities (203, 201), Relationships (300, 305)]. These steps directly address the root cause (script assigning Global) and align operational state with the blueprint (dedicated DR policy), reducing recurrence risk and restoring consistent governance over telephony routing [Data: Relationships (301, 305), Entities (194, 201)]. ## Governance and runbook recommendations — enforce blueprint and add verification The blueprint requires a dedicated routing policy for Direct Routing users; to prevent recurrence, codify that requirement in provisioning templates and add a verification step that checks assigned voice routing policies for presence of a PSTN Usage reference before enabling Direct Routing for a user [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305, 304)]. Operational runbook updates should include: a) a checklist for onboarding Direct Routing users that verifies policy assignment and PSTN Usage presence, b) an automated policy-assignment unit test in the provisioning pipeline, and c) an alert/monitor that flags Direct Routing-enabled accounts assigned to policies with no PSTN Usage. Tying these controls back to the documented blueprint will close the governance loop exposed by the incident [Data: Entities (201, 193, 194), Relationships (301, 305, 304)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 50: # Enterprise-Sales Azure AD Group Provisioning & Teams Voice Routing The community centers on Azure AD group-based automation: new Enterprise-Sales Azure AD group membership drove an automated provisioning script that assigns Teams voice routing policies; a recent provisioning-script change caused new Enterprise-Sales members to receive the incorrect "Global" voice routing policy by default and required coordination with the Identity team to correct. Azure AD Groups are used as the authoritative, single-source constructs for both voice routing policy assignment and SharePoint permissioning, governed by a dedicated group-based permissions policy; the mismatch between the provisioning logic and the documented voice-routing blueprint is the root coordination point for remediation. [Data: Entities (198,200,206,199,204); Relationships (313,309,311,312,314,762)] ## Provisioning script change caused incorrect Teams voice routing policy assignment The provisioning logic/automation is the proximate cause: a recent change to the provisioning script caused new Enterprise-Sales group members to be assigned the "Global" voice routing policy by default, and the script/logic was later modified to correct the policy assignment [Data: Entities (200)]. This behavior is explicitly tied to a mismatch between the provisioning configuration and the documented routing blueprint: the provisioning logic did not reflect the blueprint for Teams voice routing & policy assignment, prompting corrective changes to the provisioning script [Data: Relationships (313)]. ## Identity team is the primary operational stakeholder for provisioning remediation The Identity team coordinated with support to review and correct the provisioning script; records indicate the provisioning script had been modified two weeks earlier and that the Identity team collaborated to adjust provisioning logic to align with voice routing architecture [Data: Entities (202); Relationships (314); Entities (200)]. Because the Identity team participated in the fix and coordination, they are the primary stakeholder for future changes to group-driven provisioning and should be the lead for gating and validating such automation changes going forward [Data: Relationships (314); Entities (202)]. ## Azure AD groups are authoritative and drive downstream provisioning and mapping to business departments Azure AD (Entra ID) group objects were created during the reorganization and are the membership source consumed by provisioning logic that assigns Teams voice routing policies [Data: Entities (199,206); Relationships (312)]. The Enterprise-Sales Azure AD group is the directory object representing the Enterprise Sales department and its membership maps directly to that business unit, which in turn drives provisioning behavior (assignment of voice routing policies) [Data: Entities (198,204); Relationships (309,311)]. ## Dedicated group-based SharePoint permissions mandate introduces governance constraints There is an explicit governance requirement that SharePoint permissions be granted exclusively via dedicated Azure AD (Entra ID) groups (SharePoint resource groups), with defined naming and lifecycle constraints [Data: Entities (470); Entities (206)]. The directory construct AZURE AD GROUPS is tied to this policy, indicating that group creation and lifecycle processes must satisfy both voice-routing and SharePoint permissioning requirements where the same group model is used [Data: Relationships (762); Entities (206)]. ## Shared use of Azure AD groups for multiple controls (voice routing + SharePoint permissions) creates cross-domain risk The environment uses the same class of Azure AD groups as the single-source mechanism for both Teams voice routing policy assignment and SharePoint resource permissioning, per the group description; this creates a coupling where changes to group creation, membership, or provisioning logic can produce effects across telephony and content-access domains [Data: Entities (206)]. The provisioning script error that affected voice routing illustrates this cross-impact risk: a provisioning logic mismatch with the voice routing blueprint resulted in incorrect telephony policy assignments for members of a business-mapped group, showing how a single change in identity automation can affect multiple service areas [Data: Relationships (313); Entities (200,198)]. ## Root cause is configuration drift between blueprint and provisioning automation The corrective action was triggered because the provisioning logic did not reflect the documented Teams voice routing blueprint, indicating configuration drift between the authoritative blueprint and the implemented automation [Data: Relationships (313); Entities (200)]. This implies gaps in change-management practices for automation—either the blueprint updates were not applied to the automation, or the automation changed without a blueprint-aligned review—necessitating formalized validation of automation against blueprints before deployment [Data: Relationships (313); Entities (200)]. ## Immediate operational priorities and recommended next steps Priority remediations should focus on (a) reconciling the provisioning automation with the voice-routing blueprint, (b) establishing a gated change-control/approval workflow between Identity and owners of the blueprint, and (c) adding verification checks for group-to-policy mappings prior to production rollout—each of these actions is directly motivated by the provisioning script misassignment and the Identity team's role in correcting it [Data: Relationships (313,314); Entities (200,202,206)]. Additionally, because Azure AD groups serve both voice-routing and SharePoint permissioning use-cases, apply an inventory and impact-analysis of existing groups to identify where separation of intent (resource groups vs functional groups) would reduce cross-domain risk, aligned with the group-based permissions policy and naming/lifecycle requirements [Data: Entities (206,470); Relationships (762)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 41: ﻿general info & description Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company Version: 1.0 Date: 15.10.2025 Author: IT Infrastructure Status: Approved This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules. REPORT 136: # Finance AD Group Reorg — Zurich Location GPO / Printer Misassignment A reorganization moved Finance users into a newly created NEW FINANCE AD GROUP while deactivating the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP. The NEW FINANCE AD GROUP had been (inadvertently) a member of a higher-level ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP, which is scoped to apply Zurich-targeted GPOs (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01). Because of this nested membership, multiple Finance users (Basel-based) received the Zurich default printer instead of their intended device; those affected users were tested after remediation and validated as resolved. The event is rooted in AD group membership changes during a reorg and demonstrates a gap in membership validation and GPO-scoping controls. [Data: Relationships (87,89,85,91); Entities (55,56,59,54,62)] ## Root cause: nested AD group membership caused Zurich GPOs (including default printer) to apply to Finance users The NEW FINANCE AD GROUP was a member of the higher-level ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP, which resulted in Zurich-scoped Group Policy Objects (GPOs) — including the default printer mapping to MFP-ZUERICH-01 — being applied to users who were intended to receive Finance-targeted policies. The explicit link between the new Finance group and the Zurich location group is documented as the misconfiguration that allowed location GPOs to flow into Finance users' sessions. [Data: Relationships (87,91); Entities (55,56)] This is a classic nested-group GPO-scoping issue: location-scope group membership was inherited by an organizational/service-focused group, producing side effects (printer mapping) outside the intended boundary. ## Change event sequence: reorganization and group lifecycle change introduced the misconfiguration The Finance department was migrated into a newly created NEW FINANCE AD GROUP during a reorganization, and the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP was deactivated as part of that change. That migration and deactivation sequence is the proximal change event that resulted in unexpected nested membership in the Zurich location group. The timeline indicates the reorg activity as the starting point for the misapplied policies. [Data: Relationships (85,89); Entities (54,59,55)] Because the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP was removed and users were moved into the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP, any unintended parent-group memberships of the new group immediately affected the entire set of migrated users. ## Impact scope: multiple Finance (Basel) users experienced incorrect default printer assignment A set of affected Finance-department employees at the Basel location experienced their default printer automatically switching to the Zurich device. The dataset explicitly identifies these affected users and ties them to the Finance department membership that determines policy application. This shows the incident affected multiple end users within an organizational unit rather than an isolated single-user event. [Data: Entities (62); Relationships (86)] The misapplied printer mapping (Zurich device) is explicitly associated with the Zurich location GPOs, confirming the functional impact on print services for those users. [Data: Relationships (91)] ## GPO scoping is location-based and includes the MFP-ZUERICH-01 default printer assignment The ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP represents the Zurich site/scope in Active Directory and is associated with Zurich-targeted GPOs and the Zurich printer assignment (MFP-ZUERICH-01). This confirms that location AD groups are being used to scope device and printer mappings, so membership in a location group directly controls which printers users receive. [Data: Relationships (91); Entities (56)] Because those GPOs carry device mapping settings, any non-location group that becomes a member of the location group will inherit those device-targeted settings, which is what occurred here. ## Remediation validated: affected users tested and remediation resolved the issue After corrective action, the affected users were tested and validated as resolved according to the dataset. The explicit testing and validation step demonstrates that the corrective change(s) were effective for the identified set of users. [Data: Entities (62)] This confirms that the misconfiguration was functionally reversible and that remediation successfully restored expected printer mappings for the tested users. ## Evidence of governance/process gap around AD group membership and change validation The incident sequence—creation of a NEW FINANCE AD GROUP, deactivation of the OLD FINANCE AD GROUP, and the NEW group’s inadvertent membership in the ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP—indicates there was no effective pre-change validation or post-change membership audit to catch inappropriate nested membership before it impacted users. The dataset documents both the reorg move and the incorrect membership as the causal chain. [Data: Relationships (85,89,87); Entities (55,59)] While the records do not name responsible teams, the technical artifacts show a control gap: AD-group lifecycle changes that can affect location-scoped GPOs require explicit checks (membership review, test cohorts) that were not evidently performed in this case. ## Recurring risk: nested groups and implicit inheritance make similar incidents likely without controls Because the Zurich location group applies critical configuration (GPOs and default printers) and the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP was able to gain membership in that location group, the environment is exposed to future misapplied policies if group nesting is not managed. The specific relationships in the data show how a single nested membership can cause broad policy application to an entire department. [Data: Relationships (87,91)] Left unaddressed, this pattern is a recurring failure mode: organizational-group changes (reorgs, group consolidations) frequently create unexpected inheritance of location- or role-scoped policies. ## Priority recommendations: immediate actions and medium-term governance changes to prevent recurrence Immediate (urgent) actions to perform based on the incident facts: - Audit and correct membership: review NEW FINANCE AD GROUP membership and remove unintended parent/location memberships (verify Zurich membership was removed) to stop further misapplication. The incident specifically identifies the NEW group’s membership in the Zurich location group as the cause. [Data: Relationships (87)] - Verify impacted endpoints/users: confirm the remediation validated for AFFECTED USERS and run a wider scope check across Finance to ensure no remaining Basel users are receiving Zurich printer mappings. The dataset documents tested/validated affected users. [Data: Entities (62); Relationships (86)] Medium-term governance and technical controls to reduce recurrence: - Implement group-membership pre-change validation and post-change audit workflows for AD group lifecycle operations (especially for groups that can be nested into location-scoped groups). - Introduce an explicit policy for separating location-scoped groups from organizational/role groups (and prevent cross-membership) or use security filtering/WMI/GP loopback patterns as appropriate to reduce reliance on group nesting for scoping (the data shows location group membership directly controls printer mappings). [Data: Relationships (91)] - Add a change-window and pilot testing requirement for AD group restructures impacting large user sets, plus a communications plan to notify affected departments (for example, Finance) before and after changes. The reorg that created the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP is the documented change event that triggered the problem. [Data: Relationships (85,89); Entities (54)] These recommendations directly address the causal links recorded in the dataset and are prioritized to stop immediate harm, validate remediation, and close the governance gap that allowed the misconfiguration.

Activities 07.04.25 08:04 Subject: Project members (external) lose access to SharePoint From: Contact Person 07 Good morning Several external project members report that they no longer have access to the folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 Internal employees do not appear to have issues. Please review. Kind regards Contact Person 07 07.04.25 08:12 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 31 Status: In Progress Notes 07.04.25 09:05 Helpdesk asks: • Does it affect only external users? • What error message appears? • Since when exactly? • Were groups or permissions adjusted? 07.04.25 10:20 Customer responds: Error message: Access denied One user could access yesterday. Today no longer. No deliberate changes made. 07.04.25 11:10 Helpdesk checks SharePoint: • Folder permissions correct • Group “SP_Compliance_Main” contains “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • External users are members of “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” Comparison internal vs external users: No visible difference. 08.04.25 08:45 Customer adds: One external user was newly added and immediately had access. Another who has been in the project for months loses access sporadically. 08.04.25 10:15 Helpdesk tests: • Remove + re-add to group → Access temporarily restored After approx. 1 hour, no access again. 08.04.25 14:30 Hypothesis: Token / cache / Conditional Access Test: User fully logs out → logs in again → Access remains denied Hypothesis rejected. 09.04.25 09:12 Helpdesk asks: Are external users created as “Guest” or “Member”? 09.04.25 10:05 Customer: All externals are created as Guest. Some were recently synchronized from On-Prem AD to Cloud. 09.04.25 11:40 First observation: For two affected users in Entra ID portal, attribute “source” = Windows Server AD For functioning external users: “source” = Azure AD Correlation unclear. 09.04.25 14:20 Ticket escalated for in-depth analysis. Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – Identity 10.04.25 08:55 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14 In-depth analysis 10.04.25 09:40 Azure AD Connect reviewed. Observation: Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud. On-Prem version is synchronized. Cloud version was originally created manually. Result: • External guests exist only in Cloud group • SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group Same name, but different Object IDs. 10.04.25 11:25 Review of group members via Graph: Cloud group: → Contains external guests Synchronized On-Prem group: → Contains only internal users Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect. 11.04.25 08:30 Reproduction in test: • On-Prem group re-synchronized • Cloud group overwritten • External users reproducibly lose access Root Cause Two groups existed with identical name: COMPLIANCE_TEAM 1. Cloud-native group 2. Synchronized On-Prem group Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated. SharePoint referenced the synchronized version. External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group. Actions 11.04.25 – 14.04.25 • Consolidation of group structure • Old Cloud group removed • On-Prem group properly cleaned • External guests managed as Cloud-only members • Azure AD Connect full synchronization performed 15.04.25 – Monitoring Access remains stable No further inconsistencies 17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 07 The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized). Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied. The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized. Access is now stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)]
