DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route" and assigning DR-Standard restored external calling in tests. The internal blueprint specifies Direct Routing users must receive a dedicated routing policy (e.g., DR-Standard/DR-Sales), indicating the provisioning behavior deviated from the documented standard. Additionally, the Sales team experienced a separate mail flow issue for older Outlook clients that was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users, showing multiple recent configuration exceptions for the same user subset. [Data: Entities (203, 193, 194, 201, 197), Relationships (301, 303, 304, 305, 300)] ## Root cause — provisioning script assigned the Global policy lacking PSTN Usage Investigation shows the provisioning script was assigning the "Global" voice routing policy by default to users who became members of the Enterprise-Sales group; that default assignment is the misconfiguration that prevented external routing for Direct Routing-enabled users. The scripting behavior is explicitly documented in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to the PROVISIONING SCRIPT [Data: Entities (193), Relationships (301)]. The Global policy observed did not reference any PSTN Usage (including the required External-Route), so users assigned to it had no external PSTN path and outbound calls failed. This absence of PSTN Usage in the Global policy is recorded in the relationship that ties VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (193, 196), Relationships (303)]. ## Scope and impact — ~6 Sales users with Direct Routing lost outbound calling Approximately six users in the Sales team who were configured for Direct Routing could not place external PSTN calls because they were assigned the Global policy without PSTN Usage. The affected user group is explicitly identified as "AFFECTED USERS (ABOUT 6 USERS)" and is linked to Direct Routing configuration in the dataset [Data: Entities (203, 197), Relationships (300, 307)]. While the immediate count is small (~6 users), the issue affected production voice service for those users and required targeted remediation (policy reassignment/testing). The records document that these users were Direct Routing-enabled and therefore required a routing policy that provided an external PSTN path [Data: Entities (203), Relationships (300)]. ## DR-Standard provides the required PSTN route and temporarily restored calling DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route", which provides the external PSTN path necessary for outbound dialing by Direct Routing users. This is captured in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: DR-STANDARD to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]. Operational testing showed temporarily assigning DR-Standard to an affected user restored external calling, indicating the absence of External-Route in the Global policy was the proximate technical blocker to outbound calling for the impacted users [Data: Entities (194), Relationships (304)]. ## Design/standards mismatch — provisioning behavior deviated from the blueprint The internal blueprint (BLUEPRINT – TEAMS VOICE ROUTING & POLICY ASSIGNMENT – VERSION 1.3) documents that Direct Routing users must be assigned a dedicated routing policy (for example DR-Standard or DR-Sales) rather than a global/default policy. This expected assignment is explicitly referenced in the relationship between DR-Standard and the blueprint [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305)]. Because the provisioning script defaulted to the Global policy, there is a clear divergence between current automated provisioning and the documented standard — a governance and process gap that allowed the misassignment to occur [Data: Entities (193, 201), Relationships (301, 305)]. ## Recurrence risk — automation propagates the misconfiguration The provisioning script's default assignment behavior represents a systematic failure mode: any user added by that automation to the Enterprise-Sales group may receive the Global policy and therefore lack PSTN routing. The script-to-policy relationship explicitly documents this automated assignment path and is the vector for recurrence unless changed [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (193)]. Because the provisioning script is an automated control point, the misconfiguration can scale beyond the initial ~6 users if left unaddressed, making remediation of the script a higher-priority fix than ad-hoc manual reassignment for individual users [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (203)]. ## Co-occurrence of different operational issues for the same user subset The Sales team (roughly 12 employees) had a subset of about six users who experienced both the Direct Routing outbound call failure and an unrelated mail issue where outgoing mail stuck in older Outlook clients' Outbox; the mail issue was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for those affected users [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. This co-occurrence indicates the same user subset has been subject to multiple configuration exceptions or special-case changes, which suggests either rapid organizational change (reassignment to "Enterprise Sales") or gaps in templated provisioning and policy inheritance that should be reviewed to avoid repeated one-off fixes [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. ## Recommended priority actions — policy reassignment, provisioning update, and audit Immediate actions supported by the evidence: (1) Reassign affected Direct Routing users from the Global policy to a routing policy that references PSTN Usage: External-Route (e.g., DR-Standard) — this action has been shown to restore calling in tests [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]; (2) Update the provisioning script so it assigns the documented dedicated Direct Routing policy rather than Global to Enterprise-Sales members [Data: Entities (201, 193), Relationships (301, 305)]; and (3) Conduct an audit of current voice routing policy assignments for all Direct Routing-enabled users to find additional misassignments [Data: Entities (203, 201), Relationships (300, 305)]. These steps directly address the root cause (script assigning Global) and align operational state with the blueprint (dedicated DR policy), reducing recurrence risk and restoring consistent governance over telephony routing [Data: Relationships (301, 305), Entities (194, 201)]. ## Governance and runbook recommendations — enforce blueprint and add verification The blueprint requires a dedicated routing policy for Direct Routing users; to prevent recurrence, codify that requirement in provisioning templates and add a verification step that checks assigned voice routing policies for presence of a PSTN Usage reference before enabling Direct Routing for a user [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305, 304)]. Operational runbook updates should include: a) a checklist for onboarding Direct Routing users that verifies policy assignment and PSTN Usage presence, b) an automated policy-assignment unit test in the provisioning pipeline, and c) an alert/monitor that flags Direct Routing-enabled accounts assigned to policies with no PSTN Usage. Tying these controls back to the documented blueprint will close the governance loop exposed by the incident [Data: Entities (201, 193, 194), Relationships (301, 305, 304)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 41: ﻿general info & description Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company Version: 1.0 Date: 15.10.2025 Author: IT Infrastructure Status: Approved This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 64: # Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint) This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 48: # Teams Direct Routing — Sales Users, Global vs DR-Standard Voice Routing A subset of roughly six Sales users who were configured for Direct Routing experienced loss of outbound PSTN calling because a provisioning script assigned them the "Global" voice routing policy which did not reference any PSTN Usage (External-Route). DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route" and assigning DR-Standard restored external calling in tests. The internal blueprint specifies Direct Routing users must receive a dedicated routing policy (e.g., DR-Standard/DR-Sales), indicating the provisioning behavior deviated from the documented standard. Additionally, the Sales team experienced a separate mail flow issue for older Outlook clients that was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users, showing multiple recent configuration exceptions for the same user subset. [Data: Entities (203, 193, 194, 201, 197), Relationships (301, 303, 304, 305, 300)] ## Root cause — provisioning script assigned the Global policy lacking PSTN Usage Investigation shows the provisioning script was assigning the "Global" voice routing policy by default to users who became members of the Enterprise-Sales group; that default assignment is the misconfiguration that prevented external routing for Direct Routing-enabled users. The scripting behavior is explicitly documented in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to the PROVISIONING SCRIPT [Data: Entities (193), Relationships (301)]. The Global policy observed did not reference any PSTN Usage (including the required External-Route), so users assigned to it had no external PSTN path and outbound calls failed. This absence of PSTN Usage in the Global policy is recorded in the relationship that ties VOICE ROUTING POLICY: GLOBAL to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (193, 196), Relationships (303)]. ## Scope and impact — ~6 Sales users with Direct Routing lost outbound calling Approximately six users in the Sales team who were configured for Direct Routing could not place external PSTN calls because they were assigned the Global policy without PSTN Usage. The affected user group is explicitly identified as "AFFECTED USERS (ABOUT 6 USERS)" and is linked to Direct Routing configuration in the dataset [Data: Entities (203, 197), Relationships (300, 307)]. While the immediate count is small (~6 users), the issue affected production voice service for those users and required targeted remediation (policy reassignment/testing). The records document that these users were Direct Routing-enabled and therefore required a routing policy that provided an external PSTN path [Data: Entities (203), Relationships (300)]. ## DR-Standard provides the required PSTN route and temporarily restored calling DR-Standard includes the PSTN Usage "External-Route", which provides the external PSTN path necessary for outbound dialing by Direct Routing users. This is captured in the relationship linking VOICE ROUTING POLICY: DR-STANDARD to PSTN USAGE: EXTERNAL-ROUTE [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]. Operational testing showed temporarily assigning DR-Standard to an affected user restored external calling, indicating the absence of External-Route in the Global policy was the proximate technical blocker to outbound calling for the impacted users [Data: Entities (194), Relationships (304)]. ## Design/standards mismatch — provisioning behavior deviated from the blueprint The internal blueprint (BLUEPRINT – TEAMS VOICE ROUTING & POLICY ASSIGNMENT – VERSION 1.3) documents that Direct Routing users must be assigned a dedicated routing policy (for example DR-Standard or DR-Sales) rather than a global/default policy. This expected assignment is explicitly referenced in the relationship between DR-Standard and the blueprint [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305)]. Because the provisioning script defaulted to the Global policy, there is a clear divergence between current automated provisioning and the documented standard — a governance and process gap that allowed the misassignment to occur [Data: Entities (193, 201), Relationships (301, 305)]. ## Recurrence risk — automation propagates the misconfiguration The provisioning script's default assignment behavior represents a systematic failure mode: any user added by that automation to the Enterprise-Sales group may receive the Global policy and therefore lack PSTN routing. The script-to-policy relationship explicitly documents this automated assignment path and is the vector for recurrence unless changed [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (193)]. Because the provisioning script is an automated control point, the misconfiguration can scale beyond the initial ~6 users if left unaddressed, making remediation of the script a higher-priority fix than ad-hoc manual reassignment for individual users [Data: Relationships (301), Entities (203)]. ## Co-occurrence of different operational issues for the same user subset The Sales team (roughly 12 employees) had a subset of about six users who experienced both the Direct Routing outbound call failure and an unrelated mail issue where outgoing mail stuck in older Outlook clients' Outbox; the mail issue was remediated by selectively enabling SMTP AUTH for those affected users [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. This co-occurrence indicates the same user subset has been subject to multiple configuration exceptions or special-case changes, which suggests either rapid organizational change (reassignment to "Enterprise Sales") or gaps in templated provisioning and policy inheritance that should be reviewed to avoid repeated one-off fixes [Data: Entities (197, 203), Relationships (307)]. ## Recommended priority actions — policy reassignment, provisioning update, and audit Immediate actions supported by the evidence: (1) Reassign affected Direct Routing users from the Global policy to a routing policy that references PSTN Usage: External-Route (e.g., DR-Standard) — this action has been shown to restore calling in tests [Data: Entities (194, 196), Relationships (304)]; (2) Update the provisioning script so it assigns the documented dedicated Direct Routing policy rather than Global to Enterprise-Sales members [Data: Entities (201, 193), Relationships (301, 305)]; and (3) Conduct an audit of current voice routing policy assignments for all Direct Routing-enabled users to find additional misassignments [Data: Entities (203, 201), Relationships (300, 305)]. These steps directly address the root cause (script assigning Global) and align operational state with the blueprint (dedicated DR policy), reducing recurrence risk and restoring consistent governance over telephony routing [Data: Relationships (301, 305), Entities (194, 201)]. ## Governance and runbook recommendations — enforce blueprint and add verification The blueprint requires a dedicated routing policy for Direct Routing users; to prevent recurrence, codify that requirement in provisioning templates and add a verification step that checks assigned voice routing policies for presence of a PSTN Usage reference before enabling Direct Routing for a user [Data: Entities (201, 194), Relationships (305, 304)]. Operational runbook updates should include: a) a checklist for onboarding Direct Routing users that verifies policy assignment and PSTN Usage presence, b) an automated policy-assignment unit test in the provisioning pipeline, and c) an alert/monitor that flags Direct Routing-enabled accounts assigned to policies with no PSTN Usage. Tying these controls back to the documented blueprint will close the governance loop exposed by the incident [Data: Entities (201, 193, 194), Relationships (301, 305, 304)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 41: ﻿general info & description Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company Version: 1.0 Date: 15.10.2025 Author: IT Infrastructure Status: Approved This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.

Azure AD Groups are used as the authoritative, single-source constructs for both voice routing policy assignment and SharePoint permissioning, governed by a dedicated group-based permissions policy; the mismatch between the provisioning logic and the documented voice-routing blueprint is the root coordination point for remediation. [Data: Entities (198,200,206,199,204); Relationships (313,309,311,312,314,762)] ## Provisioning script change caused incorrect Teams voice routing policy assignment The provisioning logic/automation is the proximate cause: a recent change to the provisioning script caused new Enterprise-Sales group members to be assigned the "Global" voice routing policy by default, and the script/logic was later modified to correct the policy assignment [Data: Entities (200)]. This behavior is explicitly tied to a mismatch between the provisioning configuration and the documented routing blueprint: the provisioning logic did not reflect the blueprint for Teams voice routing & policy assignment, prompting corrective changes to the provisioning script [Data: Relationships (313)]. ## Identity team is the primary operational stakeholder for provisioning remediation The Identity team coordinated with support to review and correct the provisioning script; records indicate the provisioning script had been modified two weeks earlier and that the Identity team collaborated to adjust provisioning logic to align with voice routing architecture [Data: Entities (202); Relationships (314); Entities (200)]. Because the Identity team participated in the fix and coordination, they are the primary stakeholder for future changes to group-driven provisioning and should be the lead for gating and validating such automation changes going forward [Data: Relationships (314); Entities (202)]. ## Azure AD groups are authoritative and drive downstream provisioning and mapping to business departments Azure AD (Entra ID) group objects were created during the reorganization and are the membership source consumed by provisioning logic that assigns Teams voice routing policies [Data: Entities (199,206); Relationships (312)]. The Enterprise-Sales Azure AD group is the directory object representing the Enterprise Sales department and its membership maps directly to that business unit, which in turn drives provisioning behavior (assignment of voice routing policies) [Data: Entities (198,204); Relationships (309,311)]. ## Dedicated group-based SharePoint permissions mandate introduces governance constraints There is an explicit governance requirement that SharePoint permissions be granted exclusively via dedicated Azure AD (Entra ID) groups (SharePoint resource groups), with defined naming and lifecycle constraints [Data: Entities (470); Entities (206)]. The directory construct AZURE AD GROUPS is tied to this policy, indicating that group creation and lifecycle processes must satisfy both voice-routing and SharePoint permissioning requirements where the same group model is used [Data: Relationships (762); Entities (206)]. ## Shared use of Azure AD groups for multiple controls (voice routing + SharePoint permissions) creates cross-domain risk The environment uses the same class of Azure AD groups as the single-source mechanism for both Teams voice routing policy assignment and SharePoint resource permissioning, per the group description; this creates a coupling where changes to group creation, membership, or provisioning logic can produce effects across telephony and content-access domains [Data: Entities (206)]. The provisioning script error that affected voice routing illustrates this cross-impact risk: a provisioning logic mismatch with the voice routing blueprint resulted in incorrect telephony policy assignments for members of a business-mapped group, showing how a single change in identity automation can affect multiple service areas [Data: Relationships (313); Entities (200,198)]. ## Root cause is configuration drift between blueprint and provisioning automation The corrective action was triggered because the provisioning logic did not reflect the documented Teams voice routing blueprint, indicating configuration drift between the authoritative blueprint and the implemented automation [Data: Relationships (313); Entities (200)]. This implies gaps in change-management practices for automation—either the blueprint updates were not applied to the automation, or the automation changed without a blueprint-aligned review—necessitating formalized validation of automation against blueprints before deployment [Data: Relationships (313); Entities (200)]. ## Immediate operational priorities and recommended next steps Priority remediations should focus on (a) reconciling the provisioning automation with the voice-routing blueprint, (b) establishing a gated change-control/approval workflow between Identity and owners of the blueprint, and (c) adding verification checks for group-to-policy mappings prior to production rollout—each of these actions is directly motivated by the provisioning script misassignment and the Identity team's role in correcting it [Data: Relationships (313,314); Entities (200,202,206)]. Additionally, because Azure AD groups serve both voice-routing and SharePoint permissioning use-cases, apply an inventory and impact-analysis of existing groups to identify where separation of intent (resource groups vs functional groups) would reduce cross-domain risk, aligned with the group-based permissions policy and naming/lifecycle requirements [Data: Entities (206,470); Relationships (762)].

Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)]

The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit. REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

Another who has been in the project for months loses access sporadically. 08.04.25 10:15 Helpdesk tests: • Remove + re-add to group → Access temporarily restored After approx. 1 hour, no access again. 08.04.25 14:30 Hypothesis: Token / cache / Conditional Access Test: User fully logs out → logs in again → Access remains denied Hypothesis rejected. 09.04.25 09:12 Helpdesk asks: Are external users created as “Guest” or “Member”? 09.04.25 10:05 Customer: All externals are created as Guest. Some were recently synchronized from On-Prem AD to Cloud. 09.04.25 11:40 First observation: For two affected users in Entra ID portal, attribute “source” = Windows Server AD For functioning external users: “source” = Azure AD Correlation unclear. 09.04.25 14:20 Ticket escalated for in-depth analysis. Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – Identity 10.04.25 08:55 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14 In-depth analysis 10.04.25 09:40 Azure AD Connect reviewed. Observation: Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud. On-Prem version is synchronized. Cloud version was originally created manually. Result: • External guests exist only in Cloud group • SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group Same name, but different Object IDs. 10.04.25 11:25 Review of group members via Graph: Cloud group: → Contains external guests Synchronized On-Prem group: → Contains only internal users Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect. 11.04.25 08:30 Reproduction in test: • On-Prem group re-synchronized • Cloud group overwritten • External users reproducibly lose access Root Cause Two groups existed with identical name: COMPLIANCE_TEAM 1. Cloud-native group 2. Synchronized On-Prem group Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated. SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
